Edge of parking lot tall slim American Persimmon tree with hundreds of fruit ripen in November. Access: Public. Falling Fruit season: August-January.
312 E Duke of Gloucester St, Williamsburg, VA 23185, USA
Black walnut trees line each side of the street. Access: Public.
Lots of blackberry brambles here. Many large berries, very delicious. Seems to be thriving as of early July 2023. The patch is threaded throughout the unmowed area. Falling Fruit season: July-July.

Wild blackberry patch; not sure what kind, but they're fairly small in size, though numerous. They're growing on the road side of the ditch. Ripe as of the very end of June/early July 2023. Falling Fruit season: June-July.

Plenty of autumn-olive plants tangled around the base of trees. Not sure what their fruiting status is, I haven't yet seen them in fruiting season. Based on other plants in the area, that would be around October (not sure how long the season is). No one lives

Several pokeweed plants. FYI, do NOT forage these unless you're experienced, they can be deadly poisonous when not prepared correctly. 

Don't eat the berries, they're always poisonous.

Small pawpaw grove on both sides of the street. No sidewalks or places to park, so it's better to bike here or to part somewhere nearby and walk. Most of the trees are not fruiting (either too young or not pollinated), but there's a tree near a lightpost that
Grove of pawpaw on the side of the road (all along the corner). Not the Shenandoah variety Falling Fruit season: August-September.

Mature black walnut tree. Many nuts on the ground as of late October 2022. Access: Public.

Four mature pecan trees. Last time I visited, they seemed to be dropping nuts with the green hulls still on and few visibly ripe nuts without hulls, but the trees appear otherwise healthy. Access: Public.

Pecan overhanging the side of what is currently housing the Global Research Institute (owned by William & Mary). Nuts don't look like they're falling ripe, though. Access: Private but overhanging.

A number of trees on both sides of the bridge. The side of the marker had a few fruits, but the height of the trees means this will likely be a low-reward area and the fruit should probably be left for critters. Falling Fruit season: August-September.
Landrum Hall, 601 Landrum Dr, Williamsburg, VA 23185, USA

Clump of pawpaw trees across from Landrum hall. As of May 2023, there are a few fruiting trees. Relatively easy access, as the trees are right on the edge of a path. Access: Public. Falling Fruit season: August-September.
N Henry St at Highland Park, Williamsburg, VA 23185, USA

Small fruiting clump of trees. You have to go off of the path to reach these, but it's only maybe 20 yards from the path and through a grassy/cleared area. Unfortunately, pretty small yield this year (2023), maybe 3-4 fruits in May—best left for the wildlife.

Located near the stairs at the bend of the path around the Crim Dell. Tree is on the inside of the curve, opposite of the lamp post. Tree is too tall to pick fruit from its branches, but fallen fruit can be gathered on the edge of the path on the side closest

Persimmon tree with tons of fruit! Access: Private but overhanging. Falling Fruit season: October-November.

Younger muscadines here. Small yield so far, but keep an eye on them in years to come. Access: Public. Falling Fruit season: August-September.

Have only seen upon drive-by. Haven't got a close look yet to verify, but it's the right time of year, and the right size and color of fruit. Access: Public. Falling Fruit season: October-November.

Healthy Blackberry bush in the middle of the Rite Aid Parking lot, on a parking lot island. Due to location, I avoided eating the fruit, however, I did take a few cuttings for propagation. Falling Fruit season: June-July.

Large muscadine vine near the split of the trail. Much of the fruit is a little overhead, but there's a fair bit in reach. Keep an eye along the length of the trail, as there are some plants I hope will begin to fruit more in the next few years. Fruit-to-skin/

Persimmon tree next to the sidewalk near the light. Unfortunately I haven't been able to take a close look at this tree to verify, but the orange fruit was unmistakable on my daily commute in the fall. Access: Private but overhanging.
